food.text: Shakshuka at a Local Breakfast Spot
food.detail: Eggs poached in spiced tomato sauce, typically eaten with bread for dunking, a staple morning meal before heading out on early birding walks.

food.text: Hummus and Falafel from Port Market Vendors
food.detail: Freshly made chickpea dips and fried balls sold at Eilat's waterfront market, affordable fuel for a full day of observation.
Strong, cardamom-infused Arabic coffee paired with fresh or dried dates, offered as a welcoming gesture in the center's rest areas between tours.

culture.detail: Birders should arrive before dawn (5:00–6:00 AM) when bird activity peaks; this is standard practice and essential for prime sightings.
Wear lightweight, neutral-colored clothing that blends with the desert environment, plus a wide-brimmed hat and high SPF sunscreen for the intense Eilat sun.
While English is spoken at the center, familiarizing yourself with basic Hebrew bird names and carrying a Hebrew–English species checklist aids communication with local guides.
The birding center's peak seasons (March–May and August–October) are sacred to the local birding community; plan visits during these windows for the full experience and vibrant social atmosphere.

places.name: Aqaba Border Area
places.description: The waterfront promenade near Eilat's border with Jordan and Egypt offers views across the Red Sea to the Jordanian city of Aqaba and Sinai Peninsula. The area is historically significant and provides perspective on the region's unique geopolitical setting.

activities.detail: Stand at the northernmost point of the Red Sea where Israel, Jordan, and Egypt converge, offering unobstructed views of Aqaba's waterfront and the Sinai Peninsula across the water.

activities.text: Red Sea Snorkeling from Eilat Shore
activities.detail: Enter the water directly from the promenade to encounter coral reefs and tropical fish within meters of the Jordanian border, accessible year-round.
Stroll along the waterfront promenade designated as a peace area, featuring information plaques about regional history and cross-border cooperation initiatives.
Capture the unique sight where three nations' territories are visible simultaneously—Israeli desert, Jordanian hills, and the Sinai Peninsula to the south.

activities.text: Visit Eilat Museum of Glass Art
activities.detail: Located nearby on the promenade, this museum showcases contemporary glass art while providing views of the Aqaba border and strategic geographic position of the city.

activities.text: Watch the Sunset from Border Overlook
activities.detail: Position yourself at the waterfront in late afternoon to observe the sun setting over the Aqaba skyline across the border, a popular local ritual.

food.detail: A waterfront establishment serving fresh grilled fish with a direct view of Aqaba, specializing in sea bream and grouper prepared with Mediterranean herbs.
Traditional Palestinian-style hummus topped with pine nuts, olive oil, and locally sourced tahini, widely served in Eilat's border-area cafés.
Freshly fried chickpea patties served with tahini sauce and vegetables, a staple quick meal sold along the promenade near the border checkpoint area.
Eggs poached in spiced tomato sauce, a regional breakfast favorite available throughout Eilat's waterfront dining establishments.
Local white fish served over spiced rice with caramelized onions, a traditional preparation reflecting the area's maritime heritage and cross-cultural influences.

culture.detail: Avoid photographing military installations, checkpoints, or the Jordanian/Egyptian sides of the border; stick to public promenade areas and inform yourself of current security guidelines.
The border area is sensitive geopolitically; be respectful of security personnel, avoid discussions of politics, and understand this is an active international boundary.
Visit the waterfront early morning or evening as temperatures exceed 40°C (104°F) in summer; winter months (November-March) offer comfortable exploration conditions.

culture.text: Hebrew and Arabic in Daily Use
culture.detail: While English is spoken in tourist areas, basic Hebrew phrases are appreciated; many local menus and signs include Arabic reflecting the region's multilingual character.

restaurants.name: The Candela
restaurants.description: Upscale Mediterranean dining with innovative cuisine and an extensive wine list in a stylish setting.

restaurants.name: Last Refuge
restaurants.description: Popular casual pub and burger joint with a relaxed atmosphere, craft beers, and quality comfort food.

restaurants.name: Casa Egizia
restaurants.description: Elegant Italian restaurant with fresh pasta, seafood, and romantic seaside ambiance overlooking the lagoon.

restaurants.name: Ginger Asian Cuisine
restaurants.description: Contemporary Asian restaurant blending Thai, Chinese, and Japanese influences with sophisticated presentation.
restaurants.mustOrder: Thai green curry with jasmine rice

restaurants.name: Pastory
restaurants.description: Casual Italian pasta house known for handmade fresh pasta and authentic Italian flavors at reasonable prices.

restaurants.name: Pasha
restaurants.description: Traditional Lebanese mezze restaurant with charming decor, hummus, grilled meats, and warm hospitality.
restaurants.mustOrder: Mixed grill platter with pita and hummus

restaurants.name: Eddie's Hideaway
restaurants.description: Intimate fine-dining venue with creative international cuisine and emphasis on fresh local seafood.
restaurants.mustOrder: Red Sea grouper with seasonal vegetables

restaurants.name: Tota
restaurants.description: Casual Mediterranean bistro with fresh salads, grilled fish, and a laid-back beachside atmosphere.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled branzino with lemon and herbs

restaurants.name: Soda Bread
restaurants.description: Casual Irish-style pub and bakery serving hearty breakfast, lunch, and excellent artisanal breads.

restaurants.name: La Bohème
restaurants.description: Upscale beachfront restaurant with refined French-Mediterranean cuisine and panoramic Red Sea views.

restaurants.name: Shark Reef Restaurant
restaurants.description: Beachfront seafood restaurant with spectacular reef views, fresh catch daily, and casual elegant dining.
restaurants.mustOrder: Mixed seafood platter with grilled prawns

restaurants.name: Dolphin Reef Restaurant
restaurants.description: Family-friendly beachfront spot overlooking the reef with fresh fish, seafood, and panoramic sea views.
restaurants.mustOrder: St. Peter's fish with tahini sauce

restaurants.name: The Mizrahi Kitchen
restaurants.description: Casual, authentic Israeli-Mizrahi kitchen serving traditional Yemenite and Levantine dishes at budget prices.

restaurants.name: Red Sea Kitchen
restaurants.description: Contemporary seafood restaurant with open kitchen, fresh daily catch, and scenic beach promenade location.

regions/jordan_valley.json.name: Jordan Valley
regions/jordan_valley.json.history: The Jordan Valley has been inhabited for thousands of years and served as a crucial passage between ancient civilizations. Biblical history is deeply embedded in the region, with numerous sites mentioned in scriptures and archaeological remains scattered throughout. During the Ottoman period, the valley was sparsely populated, but modern settlement intensified following the 1967 war when Israeli communities were established. Today it remains one of the most contested and politically sensitive areas in the Middle East.
regions/jordan_valley.json.culturalNotes: The Jordan Valley is defined by its stark desert landscape and the life-giving Jordan River, which has shaped settlement patterns and agricultural practices for millennia. The region maintains a blend of Bedouin pastoral traditions and modern kibbutz culture, creating a unique frontier character. Tourism here centers on biblical and historical sites rather than conventional attractions, and the valley holds profound spiritual significance for Christians, Muslims, and Jews alike.
regions/jordan_valley.json.bestTimeToVisit: November to March to avoid extreme heat in this low-lying desert valley.
regions/jordan_valley.json.languageNotes: Hebrew is the primary language in Israeli settlements, while Arabic is spoken among Palestinian communities and Bedouin populations in the valley. Useful Hebrew phrases include "Shalom" for hello and "Toda" for thank you, while "Sabah al-khair" is the Arabic morning greeting.

places.name: Dead Sea
places.description: The lowest body of water on Earth at 1,410 feet below sea level, the Dead Sea offers unique therapeutic mineral-rich waters and stunning desert landscapes. Visitors can experience the buoyant, salt-saturated waters and relax at resort facilities along the Israeli shore.

activities.detail: Experience the unique sensation of effortless floating in the world's saltiest body of water at 34.2% salinity, where your body naturally bobs on the surface.
Explore freshwater springs, waterfalls, and hiking trails through desert canyons with abundant wildlife and ancient cave dwellings.
Apply nutrient-rich mineral mud from the Dead Sea floor to your skin, then rinse off in the healing waters for therapeutic and cosmetic benefits.
Hike or cable-car up to the ancient fortress overlooking the Dead Sea with panoramic views and significant historical significance.
View facsimiles and learn about the ancient Jewish texts discovered in nearby Qumran caves, foundational to biblical scholarship.
Capture the otherworldly terrain of salt formations, mineral deposits, and stark desert beauty surrounding the lowest point on Earth.
Indulge in professional Dead Sea salt scrubs, mineral baths, and therapeutic massage at resort facilities using locally-sourced ingredients.

food.detail: A traditional Levantine spread of hummus, baba ganoush, tabbouleh, falafel, and fresh pita bread reflecting Palestinian and Israeli culinary traditions.

food.text: Grilled fish from the Dead Sea
food.detail: Fresh tilapia and other local fish caught from the Dead Sea, simply grilled with lemon and served at waterfront restaurants.
Flatbread topped with the aromatic Middle Eastern spice blend of thyme, sesame, and sumac, drizzled with premium local olive oil.
Eggs poached in spiced tomato sauce with peppers and herbs, a hearty breakfast dish common throughout the Jordan Valley region.
Sampling the therapeutic mineral water served chilled at resort spas, believed to have healing properties from the Dead Sea's unique composition.

culture.detail: The Dead Sea's high elevation and intense desert sun combined with reflective salt water require strong SPF sunscreen, hat, and sunglasses to prevent severe burns.
Do not submerge your head, avoid getting salt water in eyes or open wounds, and limit floating time to 15-20 minutes as the high salinity can irritate skin.
While swimwear is appropriate at designated beach areas, cover shoulders and knees when visiting nearby Palestinian communities or religious sites in the region.

culture.text: Hebrew and Arabic are primary languages
culture.detail: Learning basic greetings and numbers in both Hebrew (Israeli side) and Arabic (Jordanian/Palestinian areas) enhances interactions with local residents and vendors.

places.name: Ein Gedi
places.description: A lush nature reserve and kibbutz oasis in the Judean Desert featuring natural freshwater springs, waterfalls, and hiking trails with stunning Dead Sea views. It's an ideal spot for experiencing desert biodiversity and enjoying refreshing pools surrounded by dramatic rocky terrain.

activities.detail: Trek through Ein Gedi Stream to a refreshing waterfall and natural pool surrounded by towering canyon walls, one of the reserve's most popular routes.
Follow a scenic path along Ein Gedi's second major stream with multiple waterfalls, rock pools, and views across the Dead Sea to the Jordanian escarpment.
Climb the ridge trails above the oasis for panoramic views of the Dead Sea, lowest point on Earth, with the Moab Mountains visible across the water.
Cool off in Ein Gedi's natural freshwater pools fed by year-round springs, maintaining pleasant temperatures even in the scorching desert summer.
Watch for Nubian ibex, hyraxes, and desert birds along the trails, particularly in early morning or late afternoon when animals are most active.
Visit remains of a Byzantine-era synagogue and Iron Age settlement near the kibbutz, revealing Ein Gedi's long history of habitation.

food.detail: The Ein Gedi Kibbutz dining room offers abundant locally-grown vegetables and herbs from their desert gardens, featuring seasonal produce and dairy products.
Sample dates and pomegranates grown in the kibbutz orchards, ancient Judean Desert fruits that thrive in this microclimate oasis.
Fresh bread baked daily at the kibbutz bakery, often featuring whole grains and herbs that pair perfectly with local spreads and cheeses.
While not food, Ein Gedi's natural springs provide mineral-rich freshwater offering a unique contrast to the hypersaline Dead Sea just below.
Drinks made from desert herbs and plants harvested locally, reflecting traditional Bedouin and kibbutz knowledge of desert botanicals.

culture.detail: Ein Gedi is an active kibbutz community; respect private areas, ask permission before photographing residents, and visit only designated public areas and nature trails.
Start hikes early morning before 9 AM as temperatures exceed 40°C (104°F) by midday; bring 2-3 liters of water per person and wear sun protection.
While hikers wear practical outdoor gear, residents in the kibbutz area dress conservatively; cover shoulders and knees when visiting communal spaces out of respect.

culture.text: Spring Water Reverence in Desert Culture
culture.detail: Local culture deeply values the rare freshwater springs as life-giving in the harsh desert; avoid contaminating pools and respect closure areas protecting water sources.

places.name: Beit Alpha
places.description: A kibbutz featuring a remarkable sixth-century synagogue with an intact and colorful mosaic floor depicting biblical and zodiac imagery. It provides an important example of Byzantine-period Jewish religious art and architecture.

activities.detail: Walk through the remarkably preserved Byzantine synagogue featuring its original colorful mosaic floor with zodiac signs, Torah shrine, and inscriptions in Aramaic and Greek.
Examine the intricate floor mosaics depicting the zodiac wheel, biblical scenes, and geometric patterns that represent one of the finest examples of Byzantine Jewish art.
View artifacts and explanatory exhibits that provide context about the synagogue's history, the Jewish community that built it, and daily life in the Byzantine period.
Take a stroll around the kibbutz grounds to experience the historical agricultural settlement and views across the Jordan Valley toward the Jordanian highlands.
Capture images of the synagogue's architectural features, including the entrance narthex and the apse area where the sacred Torah would have been housed.

food.detail: Sample locally grown tomatoes, cucumbers, and peppers from the kibbutz's agricultural output, often prepared simply to highlight their freshness.
Experience traditional kibbutz communal dining with simple, hearty Israeli dishes like chicken, fish, and seasonal salads prepared for groups.
Enjoy eggs poached in tomato sauce, a breakfast staple at kibbutz accommodations, made with eggs from the kibbutz's own poultry.
Try this Israeli sandwich of fried eggplant, hard-boiled eggs, and tahini sauce, sometimes available at kibbutz cafes or nearby restaurants.

culture.detail: Enter the archaeological site with quiet reverence as it remains a significant religious and historical monument to Byzantine-era Jewish culture.
While not strictly enforced at the museum, wearing modest clothing that covers shoulders and knees shows respect for the sacred nature of the site.
Understanding that Beit Alpha was founded in 1922 as a socialist collective settlement enriches appreciation for how modern Israeli kibbutzim preserve ancient heritage.

places.name: Gan HaShlosha
places.description: A natural pool fed by warm springs surrounded by lush gardens and palm trees in an oasis setting within the Jordan Valley. It's perfect for swimming, picnicking, and experiencing a serene tropical-like environment year-round.

activities.detail: Immerse yourself in the naturally heated spring-fed pool (28°C year-round) that forms the heart of the park, offering refreshing swims in crystal-clear turquoise waters.
Spread out a meal beneath towering date palms and lush vegetation that create a tropical oasis atmosphere perfect for families and groups.
Explore marked walking paths through the Jordan Valley landscape that wind past the springs and offer views of the valley and nearby cliffs.
Capture the small cascading waterfall created by the spring discharge, particularly photogenic in morning light as water flows into the main pool.
Stroll through the cultivated gardens showcasing native plants and species adapted to the Jordan Valley's semi-arid climate and microclimate.
Observe migratory and resident bird species attracted to the lush spring-fed environment, particularly during spring and autumn migration seasons.

food.text: Freshly Caught Fish from the Springs
food.detail: Local restaurants near the park prepare tilapia and other freshwater fish caught from or raised in the spring waters, often grilled simply with herbs and lemon.
Pack or purchase fresh Israeli salads featuring tomatoes, cucumbers, and herbs grown in the fertile oasis soil, dressed with olive oil and za'atar.
Sample ma'amoul cookies and date paste treats made from dates harvested from the abundant palm trees throughout the valley.
Visit the kibbutz dining hall or nearby Shaar HaGolan restaurant for creamy hummus topped with pine nuts and olive oil, served with warm pita bread.

culture.detail: The springs are a precious resource in the arid valley; avoid using soap or chemicals in the water and follow all park conservation guidelines to protect this delicate ecosystem.
While not strictly enforced, many Israeli visitors wear conservative swimwear; families and religious visitors are common, so modest bathing suits are more culturally appropriate.
Weekends and holidays draw large crowds; arrive early in the day or visit on weekdays for a more peaceful experience, and check closure dates in advance.
The site is part of Kibbutz Gan HaShlosha, one of Israel's collective communities; understanding the kibbutz movement's role in Israeli settlement adds cultural depth to your visit.

places.name: Kibbutz Kinneret
places.description: One of Israel's oldest kibbutzim with historical significance and a peaceful location near the Sea of Galilee offering insight into kibbutz culture and history. The adjacent cemetery honors early Zionist pioneers and the area provides scenic walking paths.

activities.detail: Visit the historic cemetery honoring early Zionist pioneers and kibbutz founders, including Rachel Yanait Ben-Zvi and other movement leaders.
Explore the kibbutz museum documenting the founding in 1909 and the development of communal living in the Jordan Valley.
Stroll along scenic lakeside paths offering views of the Kinneret and surrounding mountains with peaceful observation points.
Walk through the kibbutz's working orchards and fish ponds to understand traditional and modern kibbutz farming practices.

activities.text: Early Morning Sunrise at the Lake
activities.detail: Experience the tranquil dawn hours by the Sea of Galilee, a favored time for reflection and photography among visitors.
Visit this small museum dedicated to Kibbutz Kinneret's artistic and intellectual heritage, showcasing local crafts and historical documents.

food.detail: Sample locally-caught St. Peter's fish (tilapia) prepared grilled or pan-fried, sourced directly from the kibbutz's fish ponds.
Experience the traditional kibbutz communal breakfast featuring fresh local cheeses, eggs, home-baked bread, olives, and seasonal fruits.
Try the kibbutz's own yogurt and fresh white cheese (gvina levana) made from milk produced on-site.
Taste fresh-picked oranges, lemons, and avocados directly from the kibbutz orchards, available seasonally at the farm shop.
Enjoy a spread of hummus, baba ganoush, fresh vegetables, and bread prepared with ingredients from the kibbutz's own gardens.

culture.detail: The pioneer cemetery is a sacred site; visitors should dress modestly and move quietly through the grounds, treating it as a place of remembrance.
Kibbutz Kinneret maintains its collective values; showing genuine interest in the community's history and pioneering legacy will be warmly received by residents.
Most kibbutz facilities and shops close by late afternoon; plan visits to museums and dining areas for morning or early lunch hours.
English signs are limited; learning a few Hebrew words or carrying a translation app will enhance navigation and interaction with locals.

places.name: Beit Yerah
places.description: An ancient archaeological site at the southern tip of the Sea of Galilee with remains from multiple periods including Bronze Age, Hellenistic, and Islamic eras. The site offers insights into early settlement patterns in the region.

activities.detail: Walk among the remains of Early Bronze Age defensive walls and towers, some of the oldest monumental architecture in the Levant dating to around 3000 BCE.

activities.text: Visit the Museum of Kibbutz Ein Harod
activities.detail: View artifacts excavated from Beit Yerah including pottery, tools, and figurines displayed in the nearby kibbutz museum that help interpret the site's history.
Study the remains of buildings and installations from the Greek period when the site served as a port settlement on the Sea of Galilee.

activities.text: Walk to the Sea of Galilee Shore
activities.detail: Descend to the waterfront from the elevated archaeological site to understand the ancient settlement's strategic location and relationship with the sea.
Observe pottery kilns and residential structures from the early Islamic era that show continuous occupation of this strategic location.

food.detail: This freshwater fish from the Sea of Galilee is traditionally prepared grilled whole or baked, representing the most authentic local catch tied to the region's fishing heritage.
Sample locally pressed virgin olive oil and cured olives from surrounding kibbutzim, reflecting millennia of agriculture in the Jordan Valley.
Enjoy fresh falafel prepared daily at Ein Harod kibbutz near the site, made with local herbs and served with tahini and fresh vegetables.
This Middle Eastern egg dish cooked in spiced tomato sauce is widely served at regional kibbutz restaurants and represents local comfort food tradition.
Taste salads featuring za'atar, mint, and parsley grown in the fertile Jordan Valley soil, often served as mezze platters at kibbutz dining facilities.

culture.detail: Stay on marked pathways and do not remove artifacts or disturb excavation areas, as Beit Yerah remains an active archaeological site with ongoing research.
Time your visit to coordinate with kibbutz dining hours if you wish to eat locally, as meals are often served at set times and may require advance notice for visitors.
The Jordan Valley is extremely hot and arid with minimal shade; carry adequate water, sunscreen, and a hat as there are few facilities on the open archaeological site.
Understanding the site's Bronze Age, Hellenistic, and Islamic periods enriches the experience, as each era left distinct architectural and cultural marks still visible today.

places.name: Gesher
places.description: A small but historically significant town marking the junction of the Jordan and Yarmuk rivers, with strategic importance throughout history. The area offers views of the river junction and access to natural water features.

activities.detail: Stand at the confluence where the Yarmuk River meets the Jordan River, a strategically important waterway junction with historical significance dating back millennia.
Explore the remains of the medieval Crusader-era bridge that once crossed the Jordan River, offering insight into the region's medieval fortifications.
Walk along the valley to view ancient tell sites and layers of settlement history spanning from biblical times through Ottoman periods.

activities.text: Natural Swimming Pools at River Confluence
activities.detail: Cool off in the natural pools formed where the two rivers meet, particularly refreshing during the hot summer months.
Observe migratory and resident bird species that congregate along the Jordan and Yarmuk rivers, especially during spring and autumn migrations.
Visit the archaeological remnants of historical trading post structures that served merchants traveling the ancient trade routes through the valley.

food.detail: A traditional Jordanian lamb dish served in yogurt sauce with fresh herbs, commonly eaten in the surrounding communities of the Jordan Valley region.
Locally caught tilapia and catfish, grilled simply with lemon and herbs, reflecting the valley's riverine food culture.
Levantine salad made with parsley, bulgur wheat, and wild herbs foraged from the surrounding valley, dressed with local olive oil and lemon.
Crispy fried chickpea fritters made from legumes grown in the Jordan Valley, served with tahini sauce and fresh vegetables.
Sample the region's pressed extra virgin olive oil, produced from ancient olive groves surrounding the valley communities.

culture.detail: The Jordan River is a sensitive border area; stay within designated viewing areas and respect Israeli security protocols for river access.
Plan visits for March through May when temperatures are moderate, spring flowers bloom along the river, and water levels are optimal for viewing.
If invited to a local home or shop, expect offers of strong coffee or mint tea as a gesture of welcome; accepting is considered polite and builds rapport.
The area holds deep historical meaning for multiple cultures and religions; approach archaeological sites and monuments with appropriate respect and reverence.

restaurants.name: Pundak HaSharon
restaurants.description: Long-established local favorite serving traditional Israeli dishes in a casual, family-friendly setting.

restaurants.name: HaKipod
restaurants.address: Kibbutz Beit Alfa, Kibbutz Beit Alfa
restaurants.description: Farm-to-table kibbutz restaurant emphasizing local organic produce and contemporary Mediterranean cuisine.
restaurants.mustOrder: Seasonal vegetable tart with local cheeses

restaurants.name: Tanureen
restaurants.description: Authentic Palestinian restaurant offering traditional dishes with warm hospitality in a historic setting.

restaurants.name: Al-Reef
restaurants.description: Popular restaurant serving traditional Palestinian cuisine with outdoor seating overlooking the old city.

restaurants.name: HaGilgal
restaurants.description: Casual kibbutz dining hall offering hearty buffet-style meals with stunning Jordan Valley views.

restaurants.name: Pita House
restaurants.description: Simple roadside stand serving fresh pitas and dips, perfect for hikers and travelers.
restaurants.mustOrder: Warm pita with Israeli salad and labneh

restaurants.name: Harduf Restaurant
restaurants.address: Kibbutz Ein Harod, Kibbutz Ein Harod
restaurants.description: Upscale kibbutz restaurant showcasing organic produce and sustainable farming practices.

restaurants.name: Abu Shanab
restaurants.description: Legendary grilled meat specialist famous for perfectly charred meats and traditional sides.

restaurants.name: Bella Vita
restaurants.address: Kibbutz Tirat Zvi, Kibbutz Tirat Zvi
restaurants.description: Italian restaurant in a kibbutz setting, serving fresh pasta and Mediterranean flavors with valley views.
restaurants.mustOrder: Fresh ravioli with local ricotta and herbs

restaurants.name: Al-Quds
restaurants.description: Casual family-run restaurant offering authentic Palestinian home cooking in a welcoming atmosphere.

restaurants.name: Ein Gedi Restaurant
restaurants.address: Kibbutz Ein Gedi, Kibbutz Ein Gedi
restaurants.description: Kibbutz dining offering fresh fish and local products with views of the Dead Sea and salt flats.
restaurants.mustOrder: Tilapia grilled with olive oil and lemon

restaurants.name: Hacienda
restaurants.address: Kibbutz Ma'ale Gilboa, Kibbutz Ma'ale Gilboa
restaurants.description: Contemporary Mediterranean restaurant set in a mountaintop kibbutz with panoramic Jordan Valley vistas.
restaurants.mustOrder: Grilled lamb chops with seasonal herbs

restaurants.name: Turquoise Café
restaurants.description: Casual café near the Dead Sea offering light meals and refreshments for spa and resort visitors.
restaurants.mustOrder: Fresh salads and fish of the day

restaurants.name: Hisham's Palace Restaurant
restaurants.description: Historic restaurant near Hisham's Palace ruins serving traditional Palestinian cuisine with archaeological context.

regions.name: Galilee
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to May or September to October for pleasant weather and wildflowers.

regions.name: Golan Heights
regions.bestTimeToVisit: May to September for hiking and panoramic views of the surrounding valleys.

regions.name: West Bank
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April to avoid extreme heat and enjoy pleasant autumn and spring weather.

regions.name: Mediterranean Coast
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to May or September to November for warm weather without intense summer heat.

regions.name: Sharon
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to May or September to October for ideal beach and coastal weather.

regions.name: Samaria
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to May or September to October for comfortable exploration of historical sites.

regions.name: Judea
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April to experience the cooler highland climate and avoid summer extremes.

regions.name: Jerusalem Hills
regions.bestTimeToVisit: April to May or September to October for pleasant weather to explore the ancient city.

regions.name: Dead Sea Region
regions.bestTimeToVisit: November to March to avoid the extreme heat of this lowest point on Earth.

regions.name: Negev
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April for desert exploration with cooler temperatures and clear skies.

regions.name: Eilat & Red Sea
regions.bestTimeToVisit: October to April for comfortable diving and snorkeling with pleasant weather.

regions.name: Jordan Valley
regions.bestTimeToVisit: November to March to avoid extreme heat in this low-lying desert valley.

top10.name: Jerusalem Old City
top10.description: The ancient walled heart of Jerusalem containing sacred sites for Judaism, Christianity, and Islam including the Western Wall, Church of the Holy Sepulchre, and Dome of the Rock. It is the most historically significant and spiritually important destination in the region, drawing millions of pilgrims and visitors annually.

top10.name: Dead Sea
top10.description: The lowest body of water on Earth at 1,410 feet below sea level, the Dead Sea offers unique therapeutic mineral-rich waters and stunning desert landscapes. Visitors can experience the buoyant, salt-saturated waters and relax at resort facilities along the Israeli shore.

top10.name: Tel Aviv
top10.description: Israel's vibrant modern metropolis featuring a 1920s Bauhaus-style old city, thriving beaches, and cosmopolitan culture. Essential for experiencing contemporary Israeli life, nightlife, dining, and the famous Tel Aviv boardwalk.

top10.name: Masada
top10.description: An ancient fortress perched 1,300 feet above the Dead Sea with profound historical significance as the site of Jewish resistance during the Roman siege in 73 CE. Visitors can explore the remarkably preserved ruins, take the cable car or hike the Snake Path, and enjoy sweeping panoramic views of the Dead Sea and surrounding desert.

top10.name: Eilat
top10.description: Israel's southernmost resort city on the Red Sea, famous for world-class snorkeling, diving, and beaches with vibrant coral reefs and tropical fish. It offers a complete contrast to the desert with water sports, luxury hotels, and a lively nightlife scene.

top10.name: Sea of Galilee
top10.description: Israel's largest freshwater lake and a biblical landmark where Jesus is said to have walked on water and performed miracles. The serene waters offer swimming, fishing, and boat tours with stunning views of the surrounding Golan Heights and Galilee mountains.

top10.name: Church of the Nativity
top10.description: One of Christianity's holiest sites located in Bethlehem, traditionally believed to mark the birthplace of Jesus. Christian pilgrims from worldwide visit this ancient church, making it among the most important religious destinations.

top10.name: Caesarea
top10.description: Spectacular Roman and Crusader ruins set directly on a pristine beach, featuring an impressive aqueduct, amphitheater, and harbor. A must-see for archaeology enthusiasts and those seeking seaside history.

top10.name: Safed
top10.description: An ancient hilltop city and spiritual center of Jewish mysticism, known for its narrow cobblestone streets, blue-painted buildings, and thriving artist community. The old town offers galleries, synagogues, panoramic views, and a deeply atmospheric medieval atmosphere.

top10.name: Mitzpe Ramon
top10.description: A dramatic desert town perched on the edge of the Ramon Crater, Israel's largest natural erosion crater with stunning multicolored geological formations. This is the perfect base for hiking, astronomy, and experiencing the raw beauty of the Negev's desert landscape.

guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 40 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: Tu BiShevat
festivals.description: The Jewish New Year for Trees celebrates the blooming of almond trees across Israel with tree-planting ceremonies and nature walks. Families gather to plant trees and enjoy traditional fruits and nuts.

festivals.name: Purim
festivals.description: A joyous Jewish holiday featuring costume parades, festive meals, and street celebrations commemorating the salvation of the Jews in ancient Persia. Parties and celebrations happen throughout the country.

festivals.name: Red Flower Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the blooming of Israel's iconic red anemones across the Negev desert with guided nature tours and photography expeditions. This annual springtime phenomenon transforms the landscape into a sea of red flowers.

festivals.name: Passover (Pesach)
festivals.description: One of Judaism's most important holidays commemorating the exodus from Egypt, featuring seders, festive meals, and cultural events throughout the country. Many regions offer special Passover programs and family activities.

festivals.name: Jerusalem Wine Festival
festivals.description: Showcases Israel's thriving wine industry with tastings from prestigious local wineries and educational seminars. The festival attracts wine enthusiasts from around the world to discover Israeli varietals.

festivals.name: Remember the Fallen
festivals.description: A national day of remembrance honoring fallen soldiers and victims of terror with ceremonies, parades, and memorial services throughout Israel. The country observes a moment of silence to mark the occasion.

festivals.name: Israeli Independence Day (Yom Ha'atzmaut)
festivals.description: Israel's most significant national holiday celebrating independence with fireworks, parades, concerts, and street parties across the entire country. Every city and town hosts celebrations reflecting Israeli culture and pride.

festivals.name: Shavuot
festivals.description: A major Jewish holiday celebrating the giving of the Torah with festive meals, cheese and dairy foods, and special services. Many kibbutzim host traditional harvest celebrations and cultural events.

festivals.name: Dead Sea Marathon
festivals.description: An international running event held at the lowest point on Earth featuring marathon, half-marathon, and 10K races with stunning desert views. Participants run along the Dead Sea shores in one of the world's most unique locations.

festivals.name: Red Sea Jazz Festival
festivals.description: A world-class jazz festival featuring international and Israeli artists performing on beachfront stages in the tropical resort city of Eilat. The festival draws jazz lovers from across the globe for a week of performances.

festivals.name: White Night Festival
festivals.description: Tel Aviv's summer celebration featuring free concerts, performances, art installations, and cultural events throughout the night until dawn. The entire city stays awake celebrating contemporary Israeli culture.

festivals.name: Acre Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the historic Old City of Acre with theater, music, dance performances, and art exhibitions in the ancient streets and renovated venues. The festival highlights the city's rich multicultural heritage and artistic talent.

festivals.name: Rosh Hashanah
festivals.description: The Jewish New Year marked by special prayers, festive meals with apples and honey, and family gatherings emphasizing renewal and reflection. Services and celebrations occur in synagogues and communities nationwide.

festivals.name: Yom Kippur
festivals.description: The holiest day in Judaism dedicated to atonement with fasting, prayer services, and family reflection lasting from sunset to sunset. The entire country comes to a standstill as people observe this sacred holiday.

festivals.name: Sukkot
festivals.description: A seven-day harvest festival where families build temporary dwellings (sukkot) and gather for festive meals celebrating the autumn harvest and wilderness wandering. Markets and street fairs feature special foods and traditional activities.

festivals.name: Simchat Torah
festivals.description: A joyous celebration marking the completion and renewal of the Torah cycle with dancing, music, and street parties throughout Jewish communities. Jerusalem's streets come alive with celebrations and traditional processions.

festivals.name: International Marathon
festivals.description: A major international marathon event running through Jerusalem's historic streets and neighborhoods attracting thousands of runners worldwide. The race combines athletic competition with cultural tourism through the ancient city.

festivals.name: Olive Harvest Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the traditional olive harvest season with demonstrations of traditional pressing methods, tastings of fresh oil, and cultural performances. Visitors can participate in harvesting and learn about Palestinian and Israeli olive farming heritage.

festivals.name: Hanukkah
festivals.description: The eight-day Festival of Lights celebrates the rededication of the ancient Temple with menorah lighting, gifts, festive meals, and public celebrations. Each night features lights, music, and traditional fried foods throughout the country.

festivals.name: International Film Festival
festivals.description: One of the world's leading film festivals showcasing Israeli and international cinema with screenings, premieres, and industry events throughout Jerusalem. The festival attracts acclaimed filmmakers and cinephiles from around the globe.

festivals.name: Tsfat Klezmer Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates traditional Klezmer music and Yiddish culture in the mystical hilltop city of Tsfat (Safed) with concerts, workshops, and street performances. Musicians from Israel and abroad gather to celebrate this vibrant musical heritage.

festivals.name: Negev Beer Festival
festivals.description: Showcases Israel's growing craft beer industry with tastings, food pairings, and live entertainment in the Negev desert. Breweries from across Israel present their latest creations in this celebration of Israeli brewing culture.

festivals.name: Tel Aviv Pride Parade
festivals.description: One of the largest pride parades in the Middle East attracting hundreds of thousands of participants for a colorful celebration of LGBTQ+ rights and culture. The entire city celebrates with events, parties, and cultural programs.

festivals.name: Nahal Soreq Wildflower Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the spring wildflower bloom in the Nahal Soreq nature reserve with guided botanical walks and nature photography. Visitors experience the stunning array of colorful wildflowers in their natural habitat.

festivals.name: International Jewish Film Festival
festivals.description: Presents documentary and narrative films exploring Jewish themes and Israeli culture with screenings across multiple cities including Tel Aviv. The festival features international award-winning films and Israeli productions.

festivals.name: Netanya Festival
festivals.description: A major summer festival on Israel's Mediterranean coast featuring concerts, theater, dance performances, and art exhibitions along the beach. The festival celebrates contemporary Israeli culture with events for all ages.

festivals.name: Caesarea Theater Festival
festivals.description: World-class performances including classical music, opera, and dance held in the ancient Roman amphitheater of Caesarea with the Mediterranean as backdrop. International and Israeli artists perform in this uniquely atmospheric venue.

festivals.name: Haifa Nights Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates Haifa's multicultural character with concerts, performances, fireworks, and family events throughout the waterfront and city neighborhoods. The festival highlights the city's Baha'i heritage and diverse communities.

festivals.name: Golan Wine Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the Golan Heights' world-renowned wines with tastings from local wineries, vineyard tours, and food pairings in the scenic mountain region. Visitors experience both the wines and stunning volcanic landscape.

festivals.name: Banias Waterfall Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the natural beauty of Banias waterfall with nature walks, archaeological tours, and water activities in the lush Galilee region. The festival highlights the area's natural springs and historical sites.

festivals.name: Eilat Coral Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the unique coral ecosystem of the Red Sea with diving exhibitions, marine conservation talks, and underwater photography displays. Visitors learn about protecting one of the world's most vibrant coral reefs.

festivals.name: Jaffa Spring Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates spring in the historic Old City of Jaffa with art exhibitions, street performances, food festivals, and cultural events in ancient alleyways. The festival showcases local artists and the rich cultural heritage of this ancient port.

festivals.name: Mizrahi Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates Middle Eastern and North African Jewish culture with music, dance, traditional foods, and cultural performances. The festival honors the heritage of Mizrahi Jews from Arab countries.

festivals.name: Galilee Culinary Festival
festivals.description: Showcases the diverse cuisines and local produce of the Galilee region with cooking demonstrations, tastings, and farm visits. Local chefs highlight traditional and contemporary Galilee cooking techniques.

festivals.name: Jordan Valley Date Harvest Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the date harvest season in the Jordan Valley with tasting of fresh dates, local products, and demonstrations of traditional harvesting methods. Visitors experience the region's oldest agricultural tradition.

festivals.name: Samaria Wine Route Festival
festivals.description: Explores boutique wineries and vineyards of the Samaria region with tastings, vineyard tours, and artisanal food pairings. The festival showcases the emerging wine industry in the region's rolling hills.

festivals.name: Ein Gedi Spring Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates the natural springs and oasis of Ein Gedi in the desert with hiking, nature photography, and cultural performances. The festival highlights the unique ecology and ancient history of this desert sanctuary.

festivals.name: Northern Jazz Festival
festivals.description: Features international and Israeli jazz artists performing in the scenic Galilee region with intimate venues and stunning mountain backdrops. The festival celebrates jazz in one of Israel's most beautiful natural settings.

festivals.name: Sderot Craft Beer Festival
festivals.description: A vibrant craft beer celebration featuring local and Israeli breweries with food trucks, live music, and family activities. The festival supports the growing craft beer community in southern Israel.

festivals.name: Petach Tikva Blossom Festival
festivals.description: Celebrates spring blooms throughout Israel's historic first city with floral displays, parades, and family activities. The festival honors Petach Tikva's pioneering role in Israel's agricultural development.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Israel
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 13 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Natufian Settlement
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers transition to permanent villages in the Levantine hills.
items.text: The Natufians were among the world's first semi-sedentary peoples, occupying rock shelters and open-air camps across what is now Israel, Palestine, and neighboring regions. They developed sophisticated tools for harvesting wild grains and hunting, laying the groundwork for agriculture. Evidence of ritual burials and carved bone tools suggests emerging cultural complexity. By around 8,000 BC, some groups began cultivating wheat and barley, marking humanity's first experiments with farming.

items.label: Bronze Age Kingdoms
items.summary: City-states and kingdoms rise amid trade routes and Egyptian dominion.
items.text: The Bronze Age saw the emergence of Canaanite city-states across the region, with centers like Gezer, Jericho, and Megiddo growing as trading hubs. Egypt's pharaohs extended control over much of the territory, extracting tribute and maintaining order. The archaeological record shows robust trade networks connecting Egypt, Mesopotamia, and the Aegean, with local kings navigating between great powers. By the late Bronze Age, writing systems appeared and fortified cities dominated the landscape.

items.label: Iron Age and Hebrew Settlement
items.summary: Hebrew-speaking peoples establish themselves in the highlands as Egyptian power wanes.
items.text: With Egypt's withdrawal and the collapse of Late Bronze Age civilizations, the highlands of Canaan saw the gradual emergence of Hebrew-speaking communities. The Bible describes this period as one of conquest and settlement, though archaeologists debate how much population movement occurred versus cultural transition. Small villages developed into organized settlements, and the cult of Yahweh became increasingly central to community identity. By around 1000 BC, a united monarchy under David and Solomon achieved temporary prominence.

items.label: Divided Kingdoms
items.summary: Political split leaves Israel and Judah competing, then conquered by imperial powers.
items.text: Following Solomon's death, the united kingdom fractured into Israel in the north and Judah in the south, each pursuing separate foreign policies and religious practices. The northern Kingdom of Israel fell to Assyria in 722 BC, its people scattered or deported in what became known as the exile of the ten tribes. The southern Kingdom of Judah survived longer but faced relentless pressure from Babylon. In 586 BC, King Nebuchadnezzar II destroyed Jerusalem's temple and exiled much of the elite population to Babylon, a traumatic event that reshaped Jewish identity.

items.label: Return and Reconstruction
items.summary: Exiles return under Persian rule to rebuild temple and community.
items.text: The Persian king Cyrus the Great allowed Jews to return from Babylon and rebuild their temple, beginning a gradual restoration of Jerusalem and Judean society. The Second Temple period saw the consolidation of Jewish religious texts and practices, including the finalization of the Torah and the development of scribal traditions. Under Persian and later Hellenistic rule, a Jewish diaspora flourished around the Mediterranean while the homeland remained relatively modest in power. By the 2nd century BC, tensions between Hellenized elites and traditionalists threatened community cohesion.

items.label: Maccabean Revolt
items.summary: Jewish rebels seize independence from Hellenistic rulers, establishing the Hasmonean dynasty.
items.text: The Syrian-Greek Seleucid king Antiochus IV's attempt to suppress Jewish religious practices sparked the Maccabee family's armed rebellion, an uprising celebrated for defying a vastly more powerful empire. After initial defeats, the rebels achieved stunning military victories, reclaimed Jerusalem, and rededicated the temple—an event commemorated by Hanukkah. The Hasmonean dynasty that emerged became increasingly Hellenized and authoritarian, eventually tearing itself apart through civil war. This instability invited Roman intervention, ending a brief era of Jewish independence.

items.label: Roman Occupation
items.summary: Rome conquers and rules, sparking religious ferment and devastating Jewish rebellions.
items.text: Roman general Pompey captured Jerusalem in 63 BC, incorporating Judea into Rome's eastern territories as a client state under local kings, notably King Herod. This period saw intense religious creativity: Pharisees, Sadducees, Essenes, and early Christians competed for spiritual authority. Growing resentment of Roman taxation and cultural imperialism exploded into the First Jewish War (66–70 AD), which ended with Roman legions destroying the Second Temple and massacring its defenders. A second uprising under Bar Kokhba (132–135 AD) was equally crushed, leaving the Jewish community traumatized and dispersed.

items.label: Late Antiquity and Early Islam
items.summary: Byzantine Christian rule replaces Rome; Jewish communities endure in diaspora and small pockets.
items.text: After Rome's fall, the Byzantine Empire inherited the region and gradually Christianized it, building churches and suppressing pagan and Jewish communities. Jewish life persisted in scattered communities, with the Talmud's completion in Babylon becoming the central religious authority. Byzantine rule was inconsistent toward Jews—sometimes tolerant, sometimes persecuting—keeping them marginalized but present. The arrival of Islam in 634 AD would transform the region entirely, bringing new rulers and new circumstances for the Jewish minority.

items.label: Islamic Conquest and Medieval Period
items.summary: Arab and then Crusader armies seize the land; Jews live as protected but subordinate minorities.
items.text: Muslim armies rapidly conquered the Levant, incorporating it into an expanding Islamic empire and eventually the Umayyad Caliphate. Under Islamic rule, Jews were typically classified as dhimmis—protected non-Muslim subjects—allowed to practice their faith but subject to restrictions and special taxes. The arrival of the Crusaders (1099–1291) brought warfare and disruption, particularly devastating to Jewish communities caught between Christian and Muslim armies. After the Crusaders' expulsion, the region fell under Mamluk control, a period of relative quietude for Jewish minority communities who maintained their heritage despite marginalization.

items.label: Ottoman Rule
items.summary: Ottoman Empire administers the land for four centuries with minimal change or development.
items.text: The Ottoman conquest in 1517 brought stable but stagnant rule to the region, then known as the Province of Palestine (part of Syria). Jewish communities, swelled by Sephardic refugees fleeing Spain in 1492, concentrated in cities like Jerusalem, Safed, and Hebron, studying Kabbalah and maintaining religious traditions. The Ottoman system allowed religious minorities significant autonomy over their communities, though political and economic power remained with the Muslim majority. Little technological or economic development occurred, leaving the region rural and impoverished until European interests accelerated change in the 19th century.

items.label: Zionist Immigration and British Mandate
items.summary: European Jewish settlers arrive seeking national homeland; British rule enables Jewish institution-building.
items.text: Beginning in the 1880s, waves of Jewish immigrants from Eastern Europe arrived seeking to establish a Jewish national home, motivated by antisemitism and Zionist ideology. The British took control after World War I under a League of Nations mandate and declared support for a Jewish homeland through the Balfour Declaration, accelerating immigration and land purchases. Tensions escalated as the Arab Palestinian majority resisted what they saw as colonization, leading to periodic violence and cycles of unrest. By 1947, the region had fundamentally transformed: a robust Jewish community with schools, farms, industries, and armed militias coexisted uneasily with Palestinian Arabs who felt dispossessed.

items.label: Israeli Independence and Palestinian Displacement
items.summary: Israel declares statehood; Palestinian Arabs are driven out or remain as a minority; Arab armies fail to prevent it.
items.text: On May 14, 1948, David Ben-Gurion proclaimed the State of Israel, immediately triggering invasion by neighboring Arab states determined to prevent its survival. In the brutal 1948 Arab-Israeli War, Israeli forces defeated Arab armies and Palestinian militias, while around 700,000 Palestinian Arabs fled or were expelled, a catastrophe Palestinians call the Nakba. Simultaneously, tens of thousands of Jews from Arab lands immigrated to Israel, creating a new Jewish majority state in a region with deep Arab and Islamic roots. Israel survived, but the conflict's consequences—Palestinian refugees, resentment, and territorial disputes—would haunt the region for generations.

items.label: Occupation and Modern Conflict
items.summary: Israel seizes Palestinian territories; settlement expansion and occupation drive decades of conflict and failed peace efforts.
items.text: Israel's swift victory in the 1967 Six-Day War gave it control of the West Bank, Gaza Strip, and Golan Heights, territories populated by millions of Palestinians and Arabs. Subsequent occupation, Israeli settlement expansion, and Palestinian resistance shaped a bitter cycle of violence, intifadas, suicide bombings, and military incursions. Multiple peace efforts—Oslo Accords, Camp David, various two-state proposals—foundered on fundamental disagreements over settlements, refugees, and Jerusalem's status. Today, Israel remains a prosperous, technologically advanced democracy for its Jewish majority, while millions of Palestinians live under military occupation or in blockaded Gaza, stateless and without resolution in sight.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Israel — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Espresso is the default
items.text: When you order coffee in Israel, you're getting a small, strong espresso unless you specify otherwise. Ask for 'café hafuch' (café au lait) if you want it with hot milk, or 'americano' for a longer black coffee.

items.label: Cafés are social spaces
items.text: Israelis linger for hours over coffee, working, chatting, or reading. It's completely normal to spend an entire afternoon in a café with just one drink—no one will rush you out.

items.label: Café Hillel
items.text: A local chain beloved for strong coffee and pastries, with locations throughout Tel Aviv and Jerusalem. Skip the tourist-heavy location in the Old City and find a neighborhood branch instead.

items.label: Arrive before 9am for fresh pastries
items.text: Israeli bakeries and cafés put out their best rugelach, croissants, and burekas early; by mid-morning, selections thin out significantly.

items.label: Sabich sandwich
items.text: A breakfast or lunch staple of fried eggplant, hard-boiled egg, hummus, and tahini in pita—often eaten with coffee or as a light meal. Get it from a stand rather than a café for the best value.

items.label: Hummus & pita
items.text: Skip the supermarket versions and seek out small hummus bars in neighborhoods like Abu Tor or Mahane Yehuda Market, where it's made fresh daily and topped with warm olive oil and pine nuts.

items.label: Shakshuka
items.text: Eggs poached in spiced tomato sauce, traditionally served in cast iron with bread for dipping. It's breakfast or light dinner, and nearly every Israeli home and restaurant has their own version.

items.label: Falafel
items.text: Israel's version uses fresh herbs mixed into the chickpea batter, making it greener and more aromatic than Middle Eastern versions. Get it from a busy stand with high turnover, not a tourist trap.

items.label: Schnitzel
items.text: A breaded, fried chicken cutlet that's become nearly as Israeli as falafel—served with lemon and french fries, usually lunch food and extremely casual.

items.label: Mezze
items.text: Not a single dish but a spread of small plates: tahini, baba ganoush, Israeli salad, pickles, and olives. Order this family-style at any restaurant and you'll get the full spectrum of Israeli flavors.

items.label: Bourekas
items.text: Crispy pastry parcels filled with cheese, potato, or spinach, eaten for breakfast or as a snack. Buy from neighborhood bakeries, not tourist shops, for warm, flaky versions.

items.label: Mahane Yehuda Market [Jerusalem]
items.text: A bustling covered market packed with produce, spice vendors, and stall food. Come hungry for fresh hummus, falafel, and sabich; arrive before 2pm to avoid crowds and closed stalls.

items.label: Carmel Market [Tel Aviv]
items.text: Similar to Mahane Yehuda but more chaotic and geared toward locals. Great for cheap eats and people-watching, though watch your belongings in crowds.

items.label: Neighborhood restaurants in Neve Tzedek or Ein Kerem
items.text: Avoid the heavily touristed Old City and instead explore residential neighborhoods where restaurants serve locals at better prices and quality. Ein Kerem's winding streets have dozens of family-run spots.

items.label: Skip the Old City tourist traps
items.text: Restaurants clustered around major monuments in the Old City and near major hotels charge double and serve mediocre food. Walk 5 minutes into residential areas for authentic neighborhood spots.

items.label: Lunch is the main meal
items.text: Many restaurants offer a 'tslil yeom' (dish of the day) at lunch that's cheaper and fresher than dinner; dinner often starts late (after 8pm) and restaurants are quietest 6-7pm.

items.label: Bread and spreads come automatically
items.text: Every restaurant brings bread, hummus, and olives to the table before you order—this is complimentary, but you'll be charged if you don't eat it. It's standard practice.

items.label: Ask what's 'kasher le-Shabbat'
items.text: Many restaurants maintain kosher certification even if they're not strictly kosher in daily practice. This phrase signals a higher-quality dish and often means better ingredients were used.

items.label: Portions are large
items.text: Israeli restaurants serve generously; one main dish often feeds two people easily. Ordre mezze platters for sharing rather than individual mains if you're traveling solo.

items.label: Prices are displayed per 100g for meats
items.text: Grilled chicken and kebabs are usually listed by weight; ask for the final price before ordering if you're unsure what you'll receive.

items.label: Hebrew menu = better prices
items.text: Some restaurants print a cheaper Hebrew menu and only give tourists the inflated English version. Ask 'Yesh tafrit be-Ivrit?' (Do you have a Hebrew menu?) for local pricing.

items.label: Israeli wine is excellent and underrated
items.text: Regions like the Golan Heights and Judean Hills produce world-class wines at lower prices than European equivalents. Carmel, Tishbi, and Castel are reliable local producers available everywhere.

items.label: Arak
items.text: A strong anise-flavored spirit traditionally diluted with water (turning it cloudy) and served with mezze. It's smooth when mixed properly but deceptively strong—treat it with respect.

items.label: Beer culture is growing
items.text: Israeli craft breweries like Malka and Herzl have exploded in recent years; bars in Tel Aviv and Jerusalem now serve local IPAs and stouts alongside international brands.

items.label: Coffee, not alcohol, is the social drink
items.text: Don't expect heavy drinking culture; Israelis are more likely to spend an evening at a café than a bar. Nightlife is concentrated in specific neighborhoods and doesn't start until late (11pm or later).

items.label: Fresh-pressed juice and smoothies
items.text: Juice stands are ubiquitous in markets and on street corners; ask for pomegranate (rimon) or a mix of citrus for energizing, inexpensive refreshment.

items.label: Israelis eat directly from shared plates
items.text: Mezze is served family-style and everyone eats from the same bowls and platters using bread as utensils. It's communal and informal; don't hesitate to dive in.

items.label: Tipping is expected (10-15%)
items.text: Unlike many countries, tipping in Israel is standard for restaurants and cafés. Leave cash if possible, as card tipping systems are less common and may go unrecorded.

items.label: No negotiating prices in restaurants
items.text: Unlike markets, you don't haggle over restaurant prices. The menu price is final (though you can always ask about specials or portions).

items.label: Dinner reservations are rarely needed
items.text: Even in Tel Aviv, most restaurants operate on a walk-in basis. Arrive after 8:30pm for dinner to find a table; earlier and you'll be among tourists.

items.label: Smoking is widespread
items.text: Smoking bans exist but are loosely enforced, especially on patios and in casual restaurants. If smoke bothers you, sit indoors or leave.

items.label: Machane Yehuda Market (Jerusalem)
items.text: The city's most famous market, crammed with produce, spices, hummus, and falafel stands. Go mid-morning before the lunch rush and tourist crowds peak.

items.label: Bargain politely for produce
items.text: Market vendors expect some negotiation on fruit and vegetables, especially if you're buying multiple items. A friendly 'Too much, my friend?' often gets you a discount.

items.label: Israeli salad (salatim)
items.text: Finely diced tomatoes, cucumbers, and parsley with lemon and olive oil—appears at every meal and every market stand as a refreshing, cheap side.

items.label: Street food is safe and delicious
items.text: Hummus stands, falafel vendors, and kebab grills are high-turnover, hygenic, and cheap. Busy stalls are the safest bet for food quality.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Israel.

items.label: Israel Railways coverage
items.text: Israel Railways (רכבת ישראל) operates the main network connecting Tel Aviv, Jerusalem, Haifa, and Beer Sheva. Trains are reliable, affordable, and less congested than buses during rush hour. Book tickets online via the Israel Railways app or at station ticket offices.

items.label: Jerusalem rail service
items.text: The Tel Aviv to Jerusalem line is modern and scenic, taking about 90 minutes. Trains run frequently on weekdays but have reduced schedules on Saturdays; plan ahead if traveling on Shabbat.
Train strikes occasionally occur with little notice, particularly around wage disputes. Check the Israel Railways website or app before traveling, especially mid-week.

items.label: Discount passes
items.text: Monthly passes (קרט חודשי) offer 40–50% savings for regular commuters. Tourist passes are available but less economical unless you're taking many long-distance journeys.

items.label: Egged and Dan networks
items.text: Egged and Dan are the main bus operators covering most of Israel. Egged runs intercity and rural routes; Dan operates primarily in the Tel Aviv metropolitan area. Both accept the Rav-Kav card and cash (though cash payment is being phased out).

items.label: Rav-Kav smart card
items.text: Buy a Rav-Kav card (קרט רב-קו) at any train station, bus terminal, or convenience store and load credit. It works on all buses, trains, and the Tel Aviv metro, saving 10–15% compared to single tickets.
Bus drivers rarely give change and may refuse boarding if you pay with large notes. Use the Rav-Kav card or exact change whenever possible.

items.label: Shabbat service
items.text: No public buses run from Friday afternoon through Saturday night in most of Israel due to the Jewish sabbath. Plan accordingly and use sherut minibuses or private transport if needed.

items.label: Intercity buses
items.text: For long distances, intercity buses are cheaper and often faster than trains. Services run to Eilat, the Dead Sea, and the West Bank settlements; book via Egged's website or app.

items.label: Tel Aviv light rail
items.text: The Red Line of the Tel Aviv light rail (רכבת קלה) opened in 2023 and runs from Petah Tikva through central Tel Aviv to Bat Yam. Additional lines are under construction; the system is modern, clean, and uses the Rav-Kav card.

items.label: Jerusalem light rail
items.text: The Jerusalem light rail connects the city center to the northern suburbs via a single line. It's efficient for reaching the Old City and major stations, and accepts both Rav-Kav and contactless payment.

items.label: Haifa Carmelit
items.text: Haifa's Carmelit funicular railway ascends Mount Carmel and is mostly a tourist attraction, but it's quick and iconic. Fares are affordable and it connects downtown to the upper city.

items.label: Licensed taxis and meters
items.text: Yellow licensed taxis are ubiquitous and meters are mandatory. Always insist the driver uses the meter (מונה); if not, agree on a price beforehand or refuse the ride.
Taxi scams targeting tourists are common, especially near airports and tourist areas. Use only white or yellow licensed taxis or ride-hailing apps to avoid overcharging.

items.label: Gett and Uber alternatives
items.text: Gett is the dominant local ride-hailing app and is generally safer and more transparent than unlicensed taxis. Uber operates in Tel Aviv and Jerusalem; both apps charge slightly more than metered taxis but provide safety and receipt records.

items.label: Sherut minibuses
items.text: Shared sherut minibuses (שרות) run fixed routes and are cheaper than taxis for groups. They depart when full, making them less predictable but economical for intercity travel.
Late-night taxis may charge surcharges (often 25–30% after 21:00). Confirm the final fare with the driver before boarding.

items.label: License and documents
items.text: An International Driving Permit (IDP) and passport are required; your home country license alone may not be sufficient. Carry all documents at all times and be prepared for police checkpoints.
Israeli driving is aggressive and traffic rules are often ignored. Expect tailgating, honking, and sudden lane changes; stay alert and never assume other drivers will follow rules.

items.label: Speed cameras and fines
items.text: Speed cameras are frequent on highways and in cities; fines are steep (often 500+ NIS). Speed limits are 50 km/h in cities and 110 km/h on highways unless otherwise marked.
Driving to Palestinian territories (West Bank) is not recommended for tourists; rental companies often prohibit it. Check your insurance and rental agreement carefully.

items.label: Parking
items.text: Blue and white parking spaces require payment via apps like Pango or at meters. Parking violations result in expensive fines; use designated lots or on-street paid parking in cities.

items.label: Fuel and tolls
items.text: Petrol stations are common and fuel is relatively expensive (roughly 6–7 NIS per liter). Toll roads exist (mainly Route 6); use electronic toll devices (Viapass) or automatic billing via rental companies.

items.label: Ben Gurion Airport (TLV)
items.text: Israel's main international gateway is located 25 km southeast of Tel Aviv and serves most international flights. The airport is modern with good facilities; allow 3+ hours for international departures due to security.

items.label: Airport transport
items.text: The fastest option is the direct train to Tel Aviv (30 minutes, ~30 NIS) running from Terminal 3. Taxis and Gett are available but more expensive; rental cars are available at competitive rates.
Airport security is thorough and can be time-consuming, especially for non-Israeli citizens. Be prepared for baggage checks, questioning about your trip, and electronic device inspections.

items.label: Other airports
items.text: Eilat Airport (VDA) and Haifa Airport (HFA) handle some domestic and regional flights. Ramon Airport (near Eilat) is newer and handles some international traffic; check which airport serves your destination.

items.label: Dead Sea and Dead Sea tourism
items.text: No regular ferry services cross the Dead Sea, but private operators offer boat tours from Ein Gedi and other resorts. Tours are scenic but seasonal and weather-dependent.

items.label: Sea of Galilee cruises
items.text: Tourist boat services operate on the Sea of Galilee (Kinneret) from towns like Tiberias, offering scenic tours and fishing trips. These are tourist experiences rather than transport; book through hotels or local operators.

items.label: Red Sea diving and snorkeling
items.text: Eilat offers boat excursions to coral reefs and snorkeling sites. These are tourist activities rather than transport; operators can be booked through hotels or directly at the marina.

items.label: Rav-Kav card system
items.text: The Rav-Kav (קרט רב-קו) is Israel's unified transit card valid on buses, trains, and light rail nationwide. Available at train stations and convenience stores; load credit online or in person for discounts.

items.label: Single tickets vs. passes
items.text: Single bus tickets cost ~6–8 NIS; a monthly pass costs ~250 NIS and saves money if you travel daily. Tourists making occasional trips should simply use Rav-Kav credit or single tickets.

items.label: Tourist transport card
items.text: Some tourist centers sell tourist passes valid for 3–7 days on buses and trains in specific regions (e.g., Tel Aviv area). These are useful only if you plan frequent transit; check if a Rav-Kav is more economical.
Traveling without a valid ticket incurs a fine of 280 NIS (or up to 420 NIS if unable to show ID). Inspectors board buses and trains regularly; always carry proof of payment.

items.label: Major providers
items.text: Cellcom, Partner (Orange), and Pelephone are Israel's three main mobile networks. All offer prepaid SIM cards with data, voice, and SMS; coverage is excellent nationwide.

items.label: Getting a SIM card
items.text: Buy a prepaid SIM at the airport, convenience stores, or mobile shops; you'll need your passport. Typical starter packs include 30–100 GB of data and cost 50–100 NIS; top-up online or at shops.

items.label: Data and messaging apps
items.text: WhatsApp, Telegram, and Viber are widely used; having a local number helps for booking taxis and communicating with businesses. Most hotels and cafes offer free Wi-Fi.

items.label: eSIM option
items.text: Some international travelers use eSIM services like Airalo or local providers' eSIM plans. These avoid needing a physical card but may be more expensive than local prepaid SIMs.
Roaming charges from most countries are expensive. Purchase a local SIM immediately upon arrival to avoid unexpectedly high bills.
